title: CI/CD Workflow Health Audit
scope: All Receptor ecosystem repositories with GitHub Actions: supabase-receptor, planner-frontend, preference-frontend, workforce-frontend, match-backend, receptor-planner, website-frontend

findings.description: deno check supabase/functions/**/*.ts uses a glob that may not expand correctly on all self-hosted runner shells, potentially type-checking zero files and passing silently.

tasks.description: Replace the glob pattern in deno-check step with explicit enumeration (e.g. `find supabase/functions -name '*.ts' | xargs deno check`) or use a shell-safe glob expansion. Add a guard that fails if no files are found.

findings.description: smoke-test composite action DB canary (Check 4/5) queries /rest/v1/organisations but the canonical schema table name is orgs (confirmed). Every push to main fails the staging smoke test with a 404 after the migration was applied.

tasks.description: Update smoke-test/action.yml line 93: change the DB canary query from /rest/v1/organisations?select=id&limit=1 to /rest/v1/orgs?select=id&limit=1. Add a comment: '# table name confirmed as orgs per schema standard (260319 audit)'.

findings.description: prod-deploy.yml smoke-test-prod job calls .github/actions/smoke-test with only 2 inputs (base-url, slack-webhook-url) but the action requires 5 (base-url, anon-key, anon-jwt, incidents-webhook-url, deployments-webhook-url). Missing anon-key and anon-jwt cause PostgREST and DB canary checks to return 401, causing every production deploy to fail at the smoke test stage.

tasks.description: Update prod-deploy.yml smoke-test-prod job: add a Vault KV fetch step to retrieve anon-key and anon-jwt from secret/supabase/production/credentials, and incidents/deployments webhook URLs from secret/infrastructure/slack-incidents-webhook and secret/infrastructure/slack-deployments-webhook. Pass all 5 required inputs to the smoke-test composite action. Model the Vault OIDC auth on staging-smoke.yml pattern.

findings.description: prod-deploy.yml notify job reads secrets.SLACK_DEPLOYMENTS_WEBHOOK (no _URL suffix) via GitHub Actions secrets — bypassing Vault entirely. Canonical standard is to read from Vault path secret/infrastructure/slack-deployments-webhook for workflows with Vault OIDC access, and GitHub secret SLACK_DEPLOYMENTS_WEBHOOK_URL for runners without Vault access. prod-deploy.yml has Vault access and should use Vault.

tasks.description: Update prod-deploy.yml notify job: remove the secrets.SLACK_DEPLOYMENTS_WEBHOOK reference. Read the deployments webhook URL from Vault KV path secret/infrastructure/slack-deployments-webhook (field: url) in the same Vault fetch step as DR-03-T1. Expose as env var SLACK_DEPLOYMENTS_URL and use in the curl POST.

findings.description: prod-deploy.yml approve job depends on the GitHub 'production' environment for the manual approval gate. The 260312 audit formally deferred GitHub Environments. If the production environment has no required reviewers configured, every prod-deploy will auto-approve with no human gate — enabling unreviewed database migrations to production.

tasks.description: Configure the GitHub Actions 'production' environment in dm-ra-01/supabase-receptor Settings > Environments > production with at least one Required Reviewer (Ryan Ammendolea). Document this configuration in docs/infrastructure/ci-required-checks.md.
Verify and configure the 'production' environment in deploy-function.yml as well (it also uses 'environment: production' on the deploy job).

findings.description: deploy-function.yml uses actions/checkout@v4 and denoland/setup-deno@v2 (mutable tags) in both jobs. All other ecosystem workflows use SHA-pinned actions. Violates SEC-02.

tasks.description: Replace actions/checkout@v4 with actions/checkout@11bd71901bbe5b1630ceea73d27597364c9af683 # v4, and denoland/setup-deno@v2 with denoland/setup-deno@e95548e56dfa95d4e1a28d6f422fafe75c4c26fb # v2 in deploy-function.yml.

findings.description: deploy-function.yml has no workflow-level permissions block. The deno-check job runs on ubuntu-latest with default GITHUB_TOKEN permissions, which may include contents: write on some repo configurations. All other workflows declare permissions: contents: read (SEC-01).

tasks.description: Add 'permissions: contents: read' at the workflow level in deploy-function.yml. The deploy job requires id-token: write for any future Vault OIDC auth — add that per-job if needed.

findings.description: key-rotation-reminder.yml runs on a GitHub-hosted runner with no Vault access, so reading Slack webhook from a GitHub Actions secret is correct. However the secret name secrets.SLACK_DEPLOYMENTS_WEBHOOK_URL must be set (with _URL suffix) and documented as the canonical GitHub-secret name for runners without Vault access.

tasks.description: Ensure GitHub Actions secret SLACK_DEPLOYMENTS_WEBHOOK_URL is set in dm-ra-01/supabase-receptor repository secrets with the correct Slack incoming webhook URL for #deployments. Document the two-tier pattern in docs/infrastructure/slack-webhooks.md: (1) Vault path secret/infrastructure/slack-{channel}-webhook for Vault OIDC runners; (2) GitHub secret SLACK_{CHANNEL}_WEBHOOK_URL for platform runners.

findings.description: Unit test and build jobs in all three frontend ci.yml files still expose NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_PUBLISHABLE_KEY as the stub env var. The LIFE-05 rename changed the application variable to NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY. Unit tests run against incorrect environment configuration, so any source file reading NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY receives undefined.

tasks.description: Update planner-frontend ci.yml unit-tests and build job env blocks: rename NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_PUBLISHABLE_KEY stubs to NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY (matching the LIFE-05 application rename).
Same rename for preference-frontend ci.yml unit-tests and build jobs.
Same rename for workforce-frontend ci.yml unit-tests and build jobs.

findings.description: ci-cleanup job hardcodes NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL: http://127.0.0.1:54321 and reads SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY from ${{ env.SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY }} which is empty at job start because GITHUB_ENV is not carried across job boundaries. The cleanup Supabase client authenticates with empty credentials — test org cleanup never runs and __ci_{run_id} organisations accumulate permanently.

tasks.description: Fix ci-cleanup in planner-frontend ci.yml: move the cleanup inline into the integration-tests job as an always() step at the end (after the 'Run integration tests' step). This avoids cross-job GITHUB_ENV propagation. The Supabase instance is still running at that point and keys are available in $GITHUB_ENV from the earlier 'Start Supabase' step.
Same fix for preference-frontend ci.yml ci-cleanup job.
Same fix for workforce-frontend ci.yml ci-cleanup job.

findings.description: All GitHub Actions in match-backend/deploy.yml and receptor-planner/deploy.yml use mutable version tags (@v4, @v3, @v5, @v1) rather than SHA pins. A compromised action tag could silently execute malicious code during image builds and GitOps manifest updates. Violates SEC-02.

tasks.description: For match-backend/deploy.yml: Pin actions/checkout, docker/login-action, docker/metadata-action, docker/build-push-action, and actions/create-github-app-token to their current commit SHAs. Use `gh api repos/docker/login-action/git/ref/heads/main` or equivalent to look up SHA for each action.
Same SHA-pin updates for receptor-planner/deploy.yml.

findings.description: deploy.yml build-and-deploy job has no timeout-minutes. A hung Docker build on ubuntu-latest will consume runner minutes indefinitely. Violates CICD-05 timeout pattern established in all CI workflows.

tasks.description: Add timeout-minutes: 20 to the build-and-deploy job in match-backend/deploy.yml and receptor-planner/deploy.yml.

findings.description: website-frontend has no .github/workflows directory. There is no CI pipeline: no lint, type-check, build gate, or deploy workflow. Broken commits can merge to main with no automated validation. This is an Active repository per the ecosystem map. Deployment mechanism: Cloudflare Tunnel routing to k3s prod (same pattern as backend services).

tasks.description: Create .github/workflows/ci.yml for website-frontend with: lint job (npm run lint), type-check job (npx tsc --noEmit), build job (npm run build). Use SHA-pinned actions and arc-runner-website-frontend ARC runner label. Model on planner-frontend/ci.yml but omit Supabase, integration test, and codegen-check jobs (no Supabase dependency in website-frontend).

Create .github/workflows/deploy.yml for website-frontend. Deployment target is k3s prod via Cloudflare Tunnel (same pattern as match-backend/deploy.yml). On push to main, build Docker image, push to GHCR, update receptor-infra deployment manifest. Gate with GitHub 'production' environment approval.

findings.description: No repository has an automated rollback workflow. The rollback-runbook.md (LIFE-04) documents manual rollback only. ISO 27001 A.8.32 (Change management) requires recovery procedures — automated rollback enables faster MTTR and reduces human error in incidents.

tasks.description: Create rollback.yml for match-backend: a workflow_dispatch with input 'rollback-tag' that checks out receptor-infra, updates deployment.yaml image to the specified tag using the same sed pattern, and pushes. Gate with production environment approval.
Create rollback.yml for receptor-planner with same pattern.

Create rollback.yml for supabase-receptor DB: workflow_dispatch with target migration version input, runs supabase db push with a rollback flag or targets a specific migration tag. Gate with production environment approval.
Create rollback.yml for website-frontend with same GitOps pattern as match-backend/receptor-planner.

findings.description: Frontend repositories (planner-frontend, preference-frontend, workforce-frontend) have no deploy.yml. Deployment target is k3s prod via Cloudflare Tunnel (same pattern as backend services — build Docker image, push to GHCR, update receptor-infra GitOps manifest). Currently no workflow-as-code exists for environment promotion, approval gates, or rollback for these frontends.

tasks.description: Create .github/workflows/deploy.yml for planner-frontend. On push to main, build Docker image using the project Dockerfile, push to GHCR under ghcr.io/dm-ra-01/planner-frontend:sha, update the receptor-infra deployment manifest (same sed pattern as match-backend/deploy.yml). Gate with GitHub 'production' environment approval. Use SHA-pinned actions throughout.
Same deploy.yml for preference-frontend.
Same deploy.yml for workforce-frontend.

findings.description: The smoke-test composite action is called from both staging-smoke.yml and prod-deploy.yml. The incorrect table name (organisations vs orgs, confirmed as orgs) blocks both staging and production smoke test pipelines simultaneously. See DR-02 for the fix — this finding flags the blast radius.

tasks.description: Fix is the same as DR-02-T1. Confirm this single change unblocks both staging-smoke.yml and prod-deploy.yml smoke test paths.

findings.description: staging-smoke.yml reads Slack webhook URLs from wrong Vault paths: secret/infrastructure/slack-webhook-incidents and secret/infrastructure/slack-webhook-deployments (webhook after channel name). The canonical standard confirmed in 260319 audit is secret/infrastructure/slack-{channel}-webhook (webhook after channel). These vault kv get calls will return empty/error, so smoke test Slack notifications are silently broken and any vault kv get failure would abort the workflow before the smoke test even runs.

tasks.description: Update staging-smoke.yml lines 62-63: change path from secret/infrastructure/slack-webhook-incidents to secret/infrastructure/slack-incidents-webhook, and from secret/infrastructure/slack-webhook-deployments to secret/infrastructure/slack-deployments-webhook.

Delete orphaned Vault paths secret/infrastructure/slack-webhook-incidents and secret/infrastructure/slack-webhook-deployments using vault kv delete after the rename is confirmed. Document canonical Vault path convention: secret/infrastructure/slack-{channel}-webhook.

findings.description: prod-deploy.yml has no concurrency group. A tag push (release/*) and a simultaneous workflow_dispatch can both reach the deploy job concurrently — two parallel `supabase db push` runs against the same production DB will race on the migration lock table, potentially applying migrations twice or in undefined order. No workflow-level queuing or cancellation exists to prevent this.

tasks.description: Add a concurrency block to prod-deploy.yml at the workflow level: `concurrency: { group: 'prod-deploy-${{ github.ref }}', cancel-in-progress: false }`. Set cancel-in-progress to false (queue rather than cancel) — cancelling a mid-flight migration is more dangerous than waiting. Document that only one prod deploy can run at a time per branch/tag.

findings.description: In prod-deploy.yml (backup-pre-deploy, diff, and deploy jobs) and staging-smoke.yml (Authenticate with Vault step), VAULT_TOKEN is written to GITHUB_ENV on one line and ::add-mask:: is called on the next line. GitHub Actions processes env writes and masking sequentially — the raw token is visible in the step log between these two lines. The correct pattern is to call ::add-mask:: before echoing to GITHUB_ENV.

tasks.description: In all Vault auth steps across prod-deploy.yml and staging-smoke.yml: reorder to call ::add-mask:: before echoing to GITHUB_ENV. Pattern: `echo "::add-mask::${VAULT_TOKEN}" && echo "VAULT_TOKEN=${VAULT_TOKEN}" >> $GITHUB_ENV`. Apply to all three Vault login occurrences in prod-deploy.yml (backup-pre-deploy, diff, deploy jobs) and the single occurrence in staging-smoke.yml.

findings.description: prod-deploy.yml deploy job (lines 215–216): `git tag deployed/... || true` and `git push --tags || true` silently suppress all errors. If the deployment tag cannot be created or pushed (permissions issue, network error, SHA conflict), the job continues green with no audit record of which migrations were deployed. ISO 27001 A.8.32 requires a change audit trail — silent tag failures break it.

tasks.description: Remove the `|| true` guards from the git tag and git push steps in prod-deploy.yml. If tagging fails, the deploy step should fail rather than continue silently. If the intent is to tolerate duplicate tags (idempotency on re-run), add an explicit duplicate-tag check: test whether the tag already exists and echo a clear message rather than silently continuing.

findings.description: staging-smoke.yml lines 18 and 77 hardcode the staging base URL as `https://staging-api-829c83.commonbond.au` (an internal hash-suffixed URL). The 260319 Domain Naming Standard (§6.1 of this audit) approved `receptor-api-staging.commonbond.au` as the canonical staging URL. The smoke test targets a non-standard URL that will not exist once Cloudflare Tunnel DNS is aligned to the canonical naming standard.

tasks.description: Update staging-smoke.yml lines 18 and 77: replace `https://staging-api-829c83.commonbond.au` with `https://receptor-api-staging.commonbond.au`. Confirm prod-deploy.yml PRODUCTION_URL env var aligns with the §6.1 standard (`https://receptor-api.commonbond.au`). Document both canonical URLs in docs/infrastructure/service-urls.md.

findings.description: No CI workflow across any repository defines a concurrency group with cancel-in-progress. On rapid successive pushes (fixup commits during review), multiple CI runs queue on the shared ARC runner pool. Older runs cannot be cancelled, so the runner queue grows — new PRs wait behind stale runs consuming scarce self-hosted ARC runner capacity.

tasks.description: Add a concurrency block to all CI workflows (supabase-receptor, planner-frontend, preference-frontend, workforce-frontend ci.yml): `concurrency: { group: 'ci-${{ github.ref }}', cancel-in-progress: true }`. This cancels any in-progress CI run for the same branch when a new commit is pushed. Do not apply cancel-in-progress: true to deploy or prod-deploy workflows.

findings.description: The supabase-start composite action (line 106) writes service-role-key to GITHUB_OUTPUT via `echo "service-role-key=${SERVICE_ROLE_KEY}" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT`. GitHub Actions step outputs are not masked — even though ::add-mask:: is called on line 105, the value written to GITHUB_OUTPUT is stored in the workflow run's internal output store and is visible in the Actions UI under 'Outputs' for the step. Any caller that re-echoes or logs `steps.<id>.outputs.service-role-key` will expose the service role JWT in plaintext. The service role key bypasses all RLS policies and grants full database access.

tasks.description: Remove the `service-role-key` output from supabase-start/action.yml entirely. The service role key is already exported to GITHUB_ENV as SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY (line 99) and is usable by subsequent steps in the same job without being exposed as a step output. Remove `service-role-key` from the `outputs` declaration (lines 33–35) and from the `echo` on line 106. Update any callers that reference `steps.supabase-start.outputs.service-role-key` to use `env.SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY` instead.

findings.description: Both match-backend/deploy.yml and receptor-planner/deploy.yml run on `ubuntu-latest` (GitHub-hosted runners) with no `id-token: write` permission. This means: (1) these jobs have no Vault OIDC access — they rely solely on GitHub Actions secrets for DEPLOY_BOT_APP_ID and DEPLOY_BOT_PRIVATE_KEY, bypassing the Vault secret management standard, and (2) the GitHub App private key is stored as a plain GitHub Actions secret rather than in Vault KV. If the repository is successfully attacked (e.g. via a malicious PR with workflow access to secrets), the private key is exfiltrated and can be used to push to receptor-infra indefinitely until manually rotated. The canonical pattern for Vault-enabled repos is to store the private key in Vault KV and fetch it via OIDC at runtime.

tasks.description: Move DEPLOY_BOT_PRIVATE_KEY from GitHub Actions secrets to Vault KV path secret/infrastructure/github-app-deploy-bot. Add `id-token: write` to deploy.yml jobs, add a Vault OIDC auth step (identical to supabase-receptor prod-deploy.yml pattern), and fetch the private key from Vault before the `actions/create-github-app-token` step. Remove the GitHub Actions DEPLOY_BOT_PRIVATE_KEY secret from both repositories. Apply to both match-backend and receptor-planner deploy.yml.
Same Vault migration for receptor-planner/deploy.yml.

findings.description: match-backend/ci.yml integration-tests job starts Supabase (line 89: `supabase start --ignore-health-check`) but has no `supabase stop` step and no `if: always()` cleanup step. If the integration tests fail or the job is cancelled, the Supabase Docker containers remain running on the self-hosted arc-runner-match-backend runner. Over time, accumulated zombie Supabase containers exhaust runner disk space and port 54321 becomes occupied, causing subsequent CI runs to fail with 'port already in use' errors. Contrast with supabase-receptor/ci.yml which has an explicit `supabase stop` in an `if: always()` step.

tasks.description: Add a two-step cleanup block to match-backend/ci.yml integration-tests job, placed after 'Run integration tests' with `if: always()` on both steps: (1) `supabase stop` — tears down local Supabase containers cleanly; (2) `docker system prune -f --volumes` — removes dangling images, stopped containers, and unused volumes left by the Supabase Docker stack. Without this, each failed or cancelled run leaves ~2–3 GB of Docker layer cache and PostgreSQL volume data on arc-runner-match-backend, eventually exhausting disk and causing port 54321 conflicts for subsequent runs.

findings.description: staging-smoke.yml hardcodes VAULT_ADDR as a literal string (`env: VAULT_ADDR: https://vault.commonbond.au`) in two step env blocks (lines 47 and 58). The canonical pattern in prod-deploy.yml uses `${{ vars.VAULT_ADDR }}` (a repository variable), allowing the Vault address to be updated in one place without a code change. The hardcoded value in staging-smoke.yml will silently drift if the Vault address changes (e.g. during a migration to a new cluster), while prod-deploy.yml auto-updates.

tasks.description: In staging-smoke.yml: replace both `VAULT_ADDR: https://vault.commonbond.au` occurrences (lines 47 and 58) with `VAULT_ADDR: ${{ vars.VAULT_ADDR }}`. Confirm the `VAULT_ADDR` repository variable is set in dm-ra-01/supabase-receptor Settings > Variables. Document the `vars.VAULT_ADDR` pattern as the standard in docs/infrastructure/vault-access.md.

findings.description: Both match-backend/deploy.yml and receptor-planner/deploy.yml are named 'Deploy to Staging' but trigger on every push to main and update the receptor-infra GitOps manifest with a `staging-` prefixed image tag. Since receptor-infra drives k3s deployments via ArgoCD/Flux, a push to main in either backend repo immediately triggers a production-equivalent rollout with no approval gate, no smoke test, and no rollback checkpoint. This means 'staging' is functionally equivalent to production with no promotion barrier — a broken commit reaches the live cluster within minutes of merge.

tasks.description: Rename deploy.yml trigger to distinguish staging from production. Add a `promote-to-prod` job (or separate workflow) for match-backend and receptor-planner that requires a GitHub Environment 'production' approval gate before updating the production manifest in receptor-infra. The current deploy.yml flow should target a staging namespace in receptor-infra only. Model the two-job pattern (staging deploy → approval gate → production promote) on supabase-receptor/prod-deploy.yml.
Same staging/production promotion separation for receptor-planner/deploy.yml.

findings.description: All three frontend CI workflows use `secrets.EXTERNAL_REPO_TOKEN` — a long-lived GitHub Personal Access Token — to checkout `dm-ra-01/supabase-receptor` in at least 3 jobs per repo (integration-tests, codegen-check, e2e-axe). This is a manually rotated PAT stored as a GitHub Actions secret, bypassing the Vault secret management standard. A compromised frontend repo (e.g. via a malicious PR that reads workflow secrets) permanently exfiltrates a token with read access to supabase-receptor — including schema definitions, migration history, and function source — until the PAT is manually rotated. The canonical pattern for cross-repo checkout in this ecosystem is the GitHub App (`app-lifecycle-deployment`, ID 3127975) with scoped repository access and no permanent secret storage.

tasks.description: Replace `secrets.EXTERNAL_REPO_TOKEN` in all three frontend CI workflows with GitHub App token generation using `actions/create-github-app-token` (SHA-pinned) pointed at `dm-ra-01/supabase-receptor`. The app-lifecycle-deployment GitHub App already has this cross-repo scope. Add an `id-token: write` permission to each affected job. Remove the EXTERNAL_REPO_TOKEN PAT from all three repositories' GitHub Actions secrets after migration. Apply to planner-frontend, preference-frontend, and workforce-frontend ci.yml.

findings.description: planner-frontend/ci.yml starts Supabase with `--ignore-health-check` in three separate jobs (integration-tests line 124, codegen-check line 222, e2e-axe line 274) and calls `supabase stop` in none of them. Each job uses `--workdir supabase-backend/supabase` with no `if: always()` teardown step. On job failure or cancellation, all three Supabase stacks remain running on `arc-runner-planner-frontend`, consuming port 54321 and accumulating Docker volumes. This pattern almost certainly exists in preference-frontend and workforce-frontend ci.yml as well (same job structure). The same pattern was flagged for match-backend in DR-25, but the frontend blast radius is 3x worse — three concurrent Supabase instances per workflow run.

tasks.description: Add `if: always()` cleanup steps to integration-tests, codegen-check, and e2e-axe jobs in planner-frontend/ci.yml: (1) `supabase stop --workdir supabase-backend/supabase`; (2) `docker system prune -f --volumes`. Apply the same fix to preference-frontend and workforce-frontend ci.yml (same job structure). Consider extracting a shared cleanup composite action to avoid duplicating the stop+prune pattern across all three repos.

findings.description: smoke-test/action.yml Check 4/5 (DB canary) still queries `/rest/v1/organisations?select=id&limit=1` (line 93) and prints 'organisations table accessible' (line 104). DR-02 (raised 2026-03-19) identifies this as the wrong table name — canonical is `orgs` — and DR-16 documents the blast radius across both staging and production smoke tests. Both DR-02 and DR-16 are marked `open` with tasks assigned, but the underlying file has not been fixed. This tells us that the DR-02 task description alone (fix the path) has not been actioned. Every staging-smoke.yml and prod-deploy.yml smoke-test run is currently failing on Check 4 with a 404. This is the single highest-leverage open fix in the entire audit.

tasks.description: This is an implementation reminder, not a new fix. Immediately action DR-02-T1: in smoke-test/action.yml line 93, change `/rest/v1/organisations?select=id&limit=1` to `/rest/v1/orgs?select=id&limit=1`. Also update line 104 comment from 'organisations table accessible' to 'orgs table accessible'. This unblocks all smoke tests across staging and production simultaneously. Should be the first task actioned in Phase 1 implementation.

findings.description: The e2e-axe accessibility audit job in planner-frontend/ci.yml (and equivalents in preference-frontend, workforce-frontend) has `continue-on-error: true` (line 251). This means WCAG accessibility violations on public-facing pages are reported to the log but **never block merge**. A PR that introduces a colour-contrast failure, missing ARIA label, or keyboard navigation regression can land on main with CI green. The frontends are used by hospital staff — accessibility failures expose Common Bond Pty Ltd to disability discrimination liability under the Disability Discrimination Act 1992 (Cth) and WCAG 2.1 AA obligations. ISO 27001 A.8.25 (Secure development lifecycle) requires accessibility controls to be enforced in the pipeline, not merely observed.

tasks.description: Remove `continue-on-error: true` from the e2e-axe job in planner-frontend, preference-frontend, and workforce-frontend ci.yml. This promotes accessibility from informational to a hard merge gate. Before removing it, confirm the axe spec passes on the current codebase (run locally: `npx playwright test --config playwright.axe.config.ts`) and triage any existing violations. If violations exist, document them as known issues in a new finding rather than silently ignoring them. Consider setting an axe `impact` threshold (e.g. fail only on critical/serious) to avoid blocking on incidental moderate violations during the transition.

findings.description: planner-frontend/ci.yml unit-tests job injects `TEST_ADMIN_EMAIL`, `TEST_ADMIN_PASSWORD`, `TEST_WORKER_EMAIL`, and `TEST_WORKER_PASSWORD` from GitHub Actions secrets into the environment (lines 88–91). This job is explicitly DB-free — all Supabase calls are mocked via MSW — and these credentials are never consumed by any unit test code. They are live production/staging Supabase user credentials unnecessarily present in every unit test runner environment. Any compromised dependency, test helper, or leaked log line can exfiltrate them. The credentials should only be injected in the integration-tests job where they are actually required.

tasks.description: Remove `TEST_ADMIN_EMAIL`, `TEST_ADMIN_PASSWORD`, `TEST_WORKER_EMAIL`, and `TEST_WORKER_PASSWORD` from the env block of the unit-tests job in planner-frontend, preference-frontend, and workforce-frontend ci.yml. These secrets are only required in the integration-tests job. Verify that no unit test file imports or references these env vars (run `grep -r 'TEST_ADMIN|TEST_WORKER' src/ --include='*.unit.test.*'` to confirm). If any unit test file references them, it means the unit/integration test boundary is broken and the test should be reclassified.

findings.description: renovate.yml in all three frontend repos uses `renovatebot/github-action@v46.1.3` — a mutable semver tag, not SHA-pinned — violating SEC-02. More critically, `RENOVATE_TOKEN` is set to `secrets.EXTERNAL_REPO_TOKEN`, the same long-lived PAT targeted for deletion by DR-28. When DR-28 is implemented and the PAT is revoked, Renovate immediately breaks across all three repos with a silent auth failure. Additionally, the action runs on `ubuntu-latest` with no `permissions:` block, meaning the default GITHUB_TOKEN scope is inherited and potentially broader than needed. Renovate requires `contents: write` and `pull-requests: write` — these should be declared explicitly, not inherited.

tasks.description: SHA-pin `renovatebot/github-action` in all three renovate.yml files. Look up current SHA: `gh api repos/renovatebot/github-action/git/ref/tags/v46.1.3 --jq .object.sha`. Add explicit `permissions: contents: write, pull-requests: write` block to the renovate job. This must be done in the same PR as DR-28 to avoid breaking Renovate the moment the PAT is deleted.

Replace `RENOVATE_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.EXTERNAL_REPO_TOKEN }}` with the GitHub App token pattern — generate a token via `actions/create-github-app-token` scoped to the repos Renovate needs to open PRs against (planner-frontend, preference-frontend, workforce-frontend, supabase-receptor). Set `RENOVATE_TOKEN: ${{ steps.app-token.outputs.token }}`. This must be coordinated with DR-28 — both must land in the same release to avoid an auth gap.

findings.description: preference-frontend/ci.yml runs `supabase stop --workdir supabase-backend/supabase --no-backup 2>/dev/null || true` as the first line of every Supabase start block (lines 168, 262, 313). The `|| true` silently suppresses all errors from this pre-clean stop — including legitimate failures like 'Docker daemon not running' or 'permission denied' that would indicate a broken runner state. A runner with a broken Docker daemon would proceed past the stop, attempt to start Supabase, and fail with a confusing error that appears to be a Supabase bug rather than a runner infrastructure problem. planner-frontend and workforce-frontend have no pre-stop at all — the pattern is asymmetric across repos with no documented rationale. The `2>/dev/null || true` is a smell that this was added to paper over runner issues rather than fix them.

tasks.description: Remove the `supabase stop ... || true` pre-clean from integration-tests, codegen-check, and e2e-axe jobs in preference-frontend/ci.yml. Instead, the DR-29 fix (supabase stop in an `if: always()` teardown step) is the correct solution — it ensures stop happens after the job rather than before, and does not suppress errors. If a pre-stop is genuinely needed on this runner, it should be done without `|| true` and should log a warning rather than silently ignoring the exit code. Standardise the pattern across all three frontend repos.

findings.description: Every frontend and backend CI workflow uses `npm install` (or `pip install`) rather than `npm ci`. `npm install` can silently update `package-lock.json` when there is a version range mismatch between what is committed and what npm resolves at runtime — meaning CI may run against a different dependency tree than what developers committed and code review approved. This is a supply-chain integrity failure: a dependency could be silently bumped to a version containing a vulnerability or breaking change, and CI would still pass. `npm ci` enforces exact lockfile reproducibility, fails if `package-lock.json` is out of sync, and is the industry standard for CI pipelines. The current `npm install` usage also means the lockfile drift goes undetected until a developer runs it locally.

tasks.description: Replace all `npm install` with `npm ci` in the Install dependencies steps across planner-frontend, preference-frontend, and workforce-frontend ci.yml. `npm ci` requires `package-lock.json` to be present and up-to-date — if it is not, the build fails immediately rather than silently diverging. Run `npm install` locally first to ensure the lockfile is current before switching CI to `npm ci`. Apply to all jobs within each ci.yml (lint-and-type, unit-tests, integration-tests, build, codegen-check, e2e-axe, ci-cleanup).

findings.description: preference-frontend/ci.yml (and its counterpart in workforce-frontend) has a `ci-cleanup` job (lines 335–368) that runs as a separate job after `integration-tests` with `if: always()`. It connects to `http://127.0.0.1:54321` using `${{ env.SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY }}` to delete the `__ci_{run_id}` test org. However: (1) `${{ env.SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY }}` is not propagated across job boundaries — it was written to GITHUB_ENV in `integration-tests` but GITHUB_ENV does not carry over to separate jobs. The service role key is empty. (2) The Supabase instance started in `integration-tests` is not running by the time `ci-cleanup` executes — it is a separate job on a freshly initialised runner environment. The cleanup Supabase client connects to a dead port with empty credentials, the delete silently fails, and test orgs accumulate forever. This is the same structural defect documented in DR-10 for planner-frontend — it was not fixed when porting the pattern to preference/workforce frontends.

tasks.description: Apply the same fix as DR-10: inline the ci-cleanup logic into the integration-tests job as an `if: always()` step at the end, where SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY is still available in the environment and the Supabase instance is still running. Remove the separate ci-cleanup job. Apply to both preference-frontend and workforce-frontend ci.yml.

findings.description: planner-frontend/ci.yml integration-tests job starts Supabase with `--ignore-health-check` (line 124) and immediately reads `supabase status -o env` on the next line with no polling loop or health gate. If Kong/Auth/PostgREST are not yet ready when `supabase status` is called, PUBLISHABLE_KEY will be empty — the env var `NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_PUBLISHABLE_KEY` will be blank, and integration tests will run against an unauthenticated stack and fail with confusing auth errors rather than a clear 'Supabase not ready' message. The match-backend ci.yml (lines 93–109) has a correct 24-retry polling loop for exactly this scenario. The supabase-start composite action also has the correct health poll. planner-frontend bypasses both and goes straight to reading status — the codegen-check and e2e-axe jobs have the same gap. preference-frontend has the same issue.

tasks.description: Replace the immediate `supabase status` call in planner-frontend and preference-frontend integration-tests, codegen-check, and e2e-axe jobs with the PUBLISHABLE_KEY polling loop from match-backend/ci.yml (lines 93–109) or better yet, switch to using the supabase-start composite action from supabase-receptor/.github/actions/supabase-start (which already implements the correct health gate). This removes boilerplate from 6 jobs across 2 repos and ensures consistent, tested health-gate behaviour.

findings.description: deploy-function.yml has no top-level `permissions:` block and no job-level permissions declarations. Both jobs run with the default GITHUB_TOKEN permissions, which on most repos default to read/write for contents and metadata. The deploy job runs on `self-hosted` with `kubectl` access to the production k3s cluster — if a malicious workflow or compromised action can inherit an overly-permissive token in this context, it can write to the repo or trigger further workflows. Additionally, both `actions/checkout@v4` (lines 21, 55) and `denoland/setup-deno@v2` (line 24) are mutable semver tags, not SHA-pinned — violating SEC-02. A supply-chain compromise of either action would execute arbitrary code on the k3s production cluster runner with `kubectl` access.

tasks.description: Add `permissions: contents: read` at the workflow level in deploy-function.yml. The deploy job needs no GITHUB_TOKEN write permissions — kubectl access is via in-cluster RBAC, not the token. SHA-pin `actions/checkout@v4` and `denoland/setup-deno@v2` in both jobs. Look up SHAs: `gh api repos/denoland/setup-deno/git/ref/tags/v2 --jq .object.sha`.

findings.description: deploy-function.yml extracts the edge function name directly from the git tag using `cut -d'/' -f2` with no allowlist validation (lines 31–39). The function name is then used in `deno check "supabase/functions/${FUNCTION_NAME}/index.ts"` (line 39), `kubectl cp "supabase/functions/${FUNCTION}"` (lines 79–81), and the smoke-test curl URL (line 101). An attacker with push-tag access (any repo collaborator) could push tag `fn/../../../etc/shadow/v1.0.0` and have `deno check` or `kubectl cp` operate on an arbitrary path. The function name is also interpolated directly into a `kubectl cp` destination path — if the runner's shell is bash and the name is not sanitised, path traversal is possible.

tasks.description: Add a validation step immediately after tag extraction that checks FUNCTION_NAME against an allowlist of known edge function names (e.g. `receptor-allocator`, `match-notify`). Reject any tag where FUNCTION_NAME contains `/`, `..`, spaces, or characters outside `[a-z0-9-]`. Example: `if ! echo "$FUNCTION_NAME" | grep -qE '^[a-z0-9-]+$'; then echo '::error::Invalid function name in tag'; exit 1; fi`. Apply the same check before every downstream use of the variable.

findings.description: deploy-function.yml deploy job uses `runs-on: self-hosted` without any additional runner labels (line 47). With no labels, any self-hosted runner registered to the dm-ra-01 organisation — including runners for other repos — can pick up this job. A deploy job that runs `kubectl cp` to the production k3s cluster and performs a `kubectl rollout restart` on supabase-edge-runtime should only ever run on the dedicated `supabase-receptor` k3s runner. Without a specific label like `arc-runner-supabase-receptor`, a misconfigured or compromised runner in another repo could be assigned this job and gain production cluster access.

tasks.description: Change `runs-on: self-hosted` to `runs-on: [self-hosted, arc-runner-supabase-receptor]` (or the specific label assigned to the supabase-receptor k3s runner). Verify the label matches what is configured in the ARC HelmRelease for this runner. This ensures only the authorised runner with cluster access can execute this job.

findings.description: deploy-function.yml smoke-test step (lines 93–106) treats any non-5xx HTTP status as a pass — including 401, 403, and 404. A function that has been deployed but has a broken Deno import (causing the edge runtime to return 404 for the specific function path) will pass the smoke test and leave the runtime in a partially broken state. More critically, there is no rollback step: if the smoke test fails (5xx), the workflow exits 1 and the deployment is considered failed, but the `kubectl rollout restart` has already been executed and the pod is running the new (broken) function code. There is no `kubectl rollout undo` on failure.

tasks.description: Change the smoke-test HTTP status check to require exactly 200 or your known healthy status code (not just 'non-5xx'). Add a rollback step with `if: failure()`: `kubectl rollout undo deployment/supabase-edge-runtime -n supabase && kubectl rollout status deployment/supabase-edge-runtime -n supabase --timeout=60s`. This ensures a failed smoke-test automatically reverts to the previous pod state rather than leaving the cluster in a broken deployment.

findings.description: key-rotation-reminder.yml line 82 interpolates a GitHub Actions step output directly into a shell string that is passed to `python3 -c`: `python3 -c "import sys,urllib.parse; print(urllib.parse.unquote('${{ steps.rotation-check.outputs.alerts }}'))"`. The `alerts` output is constructed from GitHub variable values (`SERVICE_ROLE_KEY_ROTATION_DUE`, `CLOUDFLARE_TUNNEL_ROTATION_DUE`, etc.). If any of those variables contains a single quote, the shell string is broken; if it contains `'); import os; os.system(...)  #`, arbitrary shell commands execute in the context of the workflow runner. GitHub Actions variables can be set by repository admins — this is a stored shell injection vector. The correct pattern is to pass the value via an environment variable and read it in Python as `os.environ['ALERTS']`.

tasks.description: Rewrite the Slack notification step in key-rotation-reminder.yml to avoid interpolating step outputs into shell strings. Pass the encoded alerts as an env var: `env: ALERTS_ENCODED: ${{ steps.rotation-check.outputs.alerts }}` and read it in Python as `urllib.parse.unquote(os.environ['ALERTS_ENCODED'])`. This eliminates the injection surface entirely. Also validate that no GITHUB_OUTPUT values are piped through unquoted shell interpolation elsewhere in the workflow.

findings.description: key-rotation-reminder.yml uses `cron: '0 23 28-31 * *'` with a day-of-month filter applied in the step (skip if day != 01). This is a well-known GitHub Actions anti-pattern: the cron fires on days 28, 29, 30, and 31 — meaning in February (28 days), the cron fires only on day 28, then the step skips because it is not the 1st. The workflow therefore never runs in February at all unless manually triggered. A security-relevant key rotation reminder for a short month is silently skipped. The canonical fix is `cron: '0 23 L * *'` (last day of month) — but GitHub Actions does not support 'L'. The correct approach is `cron: '0 23 * * *'` with a day-of-month check, or simply `cron: '0 23 1 * *'` which fires on the 1st of each month at 23:00 UTC directly.

tasks.description: Change `cron: '0 23 28-31 * *'` to `cron: '0 23 1 * *'` and remove the 'Check if today is the 1st of the month' step entirely. The cron will fire directly on the 1st of each month at 23:00 UTC (10:00 AEST). This eliminates February's silent skip and removes the redundant day-of-month guard logic.

findings.description: key-rotation-reminder.yml monitors 4 keys: SERVICE_ROLE_KEY, PUBLISHABLE_KEY (staging), CLOUDFLARE_TUNNEL_TOKEN, and SMTP_PASS. The audit has now identified at minimum 8 additional secrets that require rotation tracking: Deploy Bot private key (DR-24), Vault root token (used during bootstrap), Codecov token (CODECOV_TOKEN), EXTERNAL_REPO_TOKEN PAT (being replaced by DR-28 but still live until then), SUPABASE_ANON_KEY (referenced in deploy-function.yml), SENTRY_DSN tokens (per-repo secrets). None of these are included in the rotation reminder. ISO 27001 A.8.24 requires all cryptographic keys to have documented rotation schedules — partial monitoring is functionally equivalent to no monitoring for the uncovered keys.

tasks.description: Extend key-rotation-reminder.yml to include all secrets identified in the audit with rotation requirements. Add corresponding GitHub Actions Variables for each new key: CODECOV_TOKEN_ROTATION_DUE, SUPABASE_ANON_KEY_ROTATION_DUE, DEPLOY_BOT_KEY_ROTATION_DUE. Document the expected rotation period for each key in key-management.md alongside the reminder config. Once DR-28 is implemented and EXTERNAL_REPO_TOKEN is deleted, remove it from the reminder config.

findings.repo: planner-frontend, preference-frontend, workforce-frontend, supabase-receptor, match-backend, receptor-planner
findings.description: No CI workflow in any repository runs secret scanning (e.g. `truffleHog`, `gitleaks`, or GitHub's native secret scanning push protection). The audit has already found credentials in awkward positions: a service-role-key written to GITHUB_OUTPUT (DR-23), a Deploy Bot private key in GitHub secrets (DR-24), and production test credentials in DB-free unit test jobs (DR-32). Without active scanning, a committed API key, Vault token, or private key would not be detected by CI — only by GitHub's passive secret scanning (which has a notification delay and only covers known patterns). ISO 27001 A.8.12 (Data leakage prevention) requires controls that actively prevent credential exfiltration at commit time.

tasks.description: Add a `secret-scan` job to the CI workflow of each repository using `trufflesecurity/trufflehog-actions-scan` (SHA-pinned) or GitHub's `secret-scanning` native push protection (enable in repo settings: Security > Code security > Secret scanning > Push protection). The truffleHog approach scans every commit in the PR diff for high-entropy strings and known secret patterns. Run it as the first job (before lint-and-type) so it fails fast. Alternatively, enable GitHub's native push protection org-wide via the org security settings.

findings.description: All frontend and match-backend CI workflows pin Supabase CLI to version 2.75.0 via `supabase/setup-cli@... version: 2.75.0`. There is no automated check that this pinned version matches the version running in the production k3s cluster. If the production Supabase CLI or server version drifts ahead of 2.75.0, integration tests may pass against a locally-started 2.75.0 schema while the production environment exposes slightly different API behaviour (e.g. changed column defaults, new RLS evaluation semantics, or schema introspection differences). The reverse is also dangerous — if 2.75.0 has known bugs, the tests validate against buggy behaviour. The supabase-receptor ci.yml uses a different version or the self-hosted CLI — that version should be the source of truth for all CI environments.

tasks.description: Create a single source of truth for the required Supabase CLI version. Add a `.supabase-cli-version` file to supabase-receptor root containing the pinned version string (e.g. `2.75.0`). Update all frontend and backend CI workflows to read this file and fail if the installed CLI version doesn't match. Alternatively, use a GitHub Actions reusable workflow or composite action for the Supabase setup step that centralises the version pin. Update renovate.json to track `supabase/setup-cli` version bumps and apply them uniformly across all repos.

findings.description: workforce-frontend/ci.yml has no `codegen-check` job and no `e2e-axe` job — unlike planner-frontend and preference-frontend which both have these jobs. workforce-frontend CI runs only: lint-and-type, unit-tests, integration-tests, build, and ci-cleanup. This means: (1) GraphQL type drift (CROSS-11) is not detected for workforce-frontend — a schema change in supabase-receptor can silently break the workforce GraphQL types without failing CI. (2) There is no accessibility audit job at all, even informational. workforce-frontend serves hospital staff who schedule and manage workforce — it is the highest operational risk app in the ecosystem and has the least CI coverage. The omission appears to be an oversight during initial CI setup rather than a deliberate decision.

tasks.description: Add a `codegen-check` job to workforce-frontend/ci.yml, identical to the one in planner-frontend/ci.yml — it checks for GraphQL schema drift against a live Supabase instance using the same pattern. Add an `e2e-axe` job (informational, with `continue-on-error: true` until DR-31 is resolved) for accessibility baseline visibility. Apply the same `if: always()` Supabase stop and Docker cleanup teardown (DR-29 fix) to the new jobs.

clarifications.item: DR-02/DR-16 — Table name in smoke-test DB canary
clarifications.decision: Resolved 2026-03-19 — canonical table name is orgs. Update smoke-test/action.yml to query /rest/v1/orgs.

Resolved 2026-03-19 — frontend apps deploy to k3s prod via Cloudflare Tunnel, same pattern as backend services (Docker image → GHCR → receptor-infra GitOps manifest). Cloudflare Pages is deferred minimum 6 months.

clarifications.item: DR-04/DR-08/DR-17 — Canonical Slack webhook Vault path and GitHub secret name
clarifications.decision: Resolved 2026-03-19 — canonical Vault path: secret/infrastructure/slack-{channel}-webhook (e.g. slack-incidents-webhook, slack-deployments-webhook). Canonical GitHub secret name for platform runners without Vault: SLACK_{CHANNEL}_WEBHOOK_URL. staging-smoke.yml was reading from wrong paths (slack-webhook-{channel}) — see DR-17.

implementationPhases.name: Critical CI/CD Failures
implementationPhases.rationale: Fix workflow defects that cause immediate failures: prod-deploy smoke-test missing inputs, wrong Vault paths in staging-smoke, wrong table name in smoke-test canary (DR-30 — action file never updated despite DR-02/DR-16), ci-cleanup broken across job boundaries for all three frontend repos (DR-36), and concurrent prod-deploy race on migration lock table. Restore all functional CI paths before security hardening.

implementationPhases.name: Security Gate — Production Approval, Website CI, and Frontend Deploy
implementationPhases.rationale: Configure GitHub Environment protection for production approval (prevents auto-approve on prod-deploy), create website-frontend CI and deploy pipeline (k8s/Cloudflare Tunnel), create deploy.yml for all other frontend repos using the same k8s pattern, and introduce staging-to-production promotion gates for backend deploy workflows.

implementationPhases.name: Secret Naming, Vault Hygiene, and Action SHA Pins
implementationPhases.rationale: Standardise Slack webhook secret references, delete orphaned Vault paths, SHA-pin all unversioned actions, fix the VAULT_TOKEN mask ordering, remove service-role-key from composite action outputs, migrate Deploy Bot private key to Vault, replace the long-lived EXTERNAL_REPO_TOKEN PAT with a GitHub App token (DR-28), and update Renovate to use the GitHub App token in the same release to avoid an auth gap (DR-33).

implementationPhases.name: Environment Variable Consistency and Supply-Chain Integrity
implementationPhases.rationale: Apply the NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY rename to CI stubs, update hardcoded staging URLs to match the Domain Naming Standard, remove production credentials from DB-free unit test jobs (DR-32), switch all frontend CI to npm ci for lockfile-exact reproducibility (DR-35), and establish a single source of truth for the Supabase CLI version across all repos (DR-46).

implementationPhases.name: Process, Governance, and Runner Health
implementationPhases.rationale: Implement automated rollback workflows, fix the Deno glob pattern, repair silent deployment tag failures, add Supabase stop and Docker disk cleanup to all affected jobs (DR-25, DR-29), remove the asymmetric supabase-stop pre-clean from preference-frontend (DR-34), add Supabase health polling to frontend jobs that skip it (DR-37), standardise VAULT_ADDR to use repository variables (DR-26), add missing codegen-check and e2e-axe jobs to workforce-frontend (DR-47).

implementationPhases.name: CI Concurrency and Runner Health
implementationPhases.rationale: Add cancel-in-progress concurrency groups to all CI workflows to prevent ARC runner queue starvation from rapid fixup commits.

implementationPhases.name: Edge Function Deploy Hardening
implementationPhases.rationale: Harden deploy-function.yml against supply-chain and infrastructure attacks: add permissions block, SHA-pin all action references (DR-38), add function-name allowlist validation against path traversal (DR-39), restrict the deploy job to a labelled runner (DR-40), tighten smoke-test success criteria and add automatic rollback on failure (DR-41).

implementationPhases.name: Governance, Observability, and Secret Inventory
implementationPhases.rationale: Fix the shell injection in key-rotation-reminder (DR-42), fix the broken February cron (DR-43), expand rotation monitoring to cover all identified secrets (DR-44), add secret scanning to all CI pipelines (DR-45).

deferred.reason: Deferred 2026-03-19 — accessibility enforcement gate agreed in principle but blocked on stable production-ready UI first. Re-evaluate once all three frontends reach their baseline quality bar. At that point, triage existing axe violations, document as known issues if needed, and remove continue-on-error.
